items.creator: UN Environment Programme
items.title: Localisation of Environment in Humanitarian Action (LEHA) Localised Training Material for Colombia
items.author: UN Environment Programme
items.content:
Country: Colombia
Sources: UN Environment Programme, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached files.

The contextualized training material for Colombia was developed based on the Global Training Template and adapted to the national context through a collaborative process, thanks to the valuable contributions of specialists in the environment and humanitarian action. The materials include a total of 11 technical modules and 1 training-of-trainers module, covering key topics related to the environment in humanitarian action (EHA). The download links include the contextualized documents in text format and ready-to-use presentations in PPT format.

  • Module 1: Introduction to the Environment in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 2: Environmental Assessment in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 3: Sustainable Waste Management in Humanitarian Contexts
  • Module 4: Sustainable Water Resources Management in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 5: Climate Change Adaptation and Disaster Risk Reduction
  • Module 6: Sustainable Land Management in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 7: Greenhouse Gas Emissions and Sustainable Energy Management
  • Module 8: Managing the Environmental Impacts of the Supply Chain and Logistics in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 9: Integrating and Financing the Environmental Dimension in Humanitarian Programs
  • Module 10: Intersectoral Collaboration and Knowledge Sharing
  • Module 11: Community Participation and Capacity Building in Humanitarian Action
  • Module 12: Training of Trainers

Training-of-Trainers (ToT) Module 12 was created to ensure the continued development of environmental capacity in humanitarian action (EHA), with a focus on building the capacity to deliver sustainable training courses.

The download links also include the Preliminary Note for the LEHA Colombia Training—a short document containing acknowledgments, an overview, and a glossary of key terms related to the content—and the Introduction to the LEHA Colombia Training, which provides a detailed description of all the components of this training and its deliverables.

This training material is open source and is available to any organization or individual for use or reference in developing and delivering training courses on the environment in humanitarian action tailored to the Colombian context.

items.creator: Insecurity Insight
items.title: Attacks on Health Care in the occupied Palestinian territory (24 June - 07 July 2026)
items.author: Insecurity Insight
items.content:
Country: occupied Palestinian territory
Source: Insecurity Insight

Please refer to the attached file.

Data collected by Insecurity Insight shows that between 07 October 2023 and 23 June 2026, at least 3,354 incidents of violence against or obstruction of access to health care took place across the occupied Palestinian territory. 95% of all incidents were attributed to Israeli forces.

  • Gaza: 2,339 attacks on health care were recorded during which health facilities were damaged 448 times, 770 health workers were killed and 398 arrested. Patient access to medicine was obstructed on 303 occasions.
  • West Bank and East Jerusalem: 947 attacks on health care were recorded during which health facilities were damaged 30 times, 12 health workers were killed and 190 arrested. Patient access to medicine was obstructed on 585 occasions.

items.creator: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.title: World: Epidemic Preparedness Ahead of El Niño: Key Lessons from the Regional Anticipatory Action Working Group for Southern Africa (RAAWG)
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Countries: World, Angola, Democratic Republic of the Congo, United Republic of Tanzania, Zambia
Sources: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, Southern African Development Community, World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

Southern Africa is bracing for a confirmed El Niño event in the upcoming season, with early projections indicating it may rival or even exceed the severity of the 1997/98 episode. Against this backdrop, the Regional Anticipatory Action Working Group (RAAWG) convened a virtual session to examine a critical but often under-addressed dimension of early action: epidemic preparedness. The discussion brought together climate scientists, public health experts, and operational responders to explore how climate forecasts can be translated into timely health interventions, ultimately shifting the region from reactive crisis response to proactive risk management.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: World, Angola, Democratic Republic of the Congo, United Republic of Tanzania, Zambia
Sources: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations, International Federation of Red Cross and Red Crescent Societies, Southern African Development Community, World Food Programme

items.creator: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.title: Yemen Agrometeorological Update: Food Security and Early Warning Information System (01 - 30 June 2026) [EN/AR]
items.author: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations
items.content:
Country: Yemen
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached files.

HIGHLIGHTS:

  • Precipitation: Rainfall across Yemen remained generally low throughout June 2026, with localized convective activity over the southern uplands. Despite this, the monthly mean was slightly above the five-year average.
  • Rainfall anomalies: Compared to the long-term average and June 2025, June 2026 showed mixed rainfall patterns across Yemen, with most areas experiencing drier-than-average conditions and some localized wetter-than-average zones.
  • Temperature: Elevated temperatures persisted across Yemen during June 2026, with daytime temperatures reaching 35–40°C in eastern and coastal regions, while highland areas remained relatively cooler with nighttime temperatures of 15–20°C.
  • Vegetation: Vegetation conditions across Yemen remained generally favorable in June 2026, with 76% of cultivated areas showing fair-to-good performance. Time-series change analysis using NDVI indicated stable or improving conditions in cultivated areas, particularly in Al Hudaydah.
  • Crop season: As the growing season advances, slight agricultural water stress (ASI: 25–40%) was observed in parts of Dhamar, Al Bayda, and Sana'a governorates, indicating localized soil moisture deficits.
  • Livelihoods and food security: Below-average rainfall combined with above-average temperatures reduced soil moisture levels, disrupting crop establishment, increasing water demand, intensifying livestock stress, and further undermining agricultural livelihoods across Yemen.
items.contentSnippet:
Country: Yemen
Source: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

Please refer to the attached files.
HIGHLIGHTS:
Precipitation: Rainfall across Yemen remained generally low throughout June 2026, with localized convective activity over the southern uplands. Despite this, the monthly mean was slightly above the five-year average.
Rainfall anomalies: Compared to the long-term average and June 2025, June 2026 showed mixed rainfall patterns across Yemen, with most areas experiencing drier-than-average conditions and some localized wetter-than-average zones.
Temperature: Elevated temperatures persisted across Yemen during June 2026, with daytime temperatures reaching 35–40°C in eastern and coastal regions, while highland areas remained relatively cooler with nighttime temperatures of 15–20°C.
Vegetation: Vegetation conditions across Yemen remained generally favorable in June 2026, with 76% of cultivated areas showing fair-to-good performance. Time-series change analysis using NDVI indicated stable or improving conditions in cultivated areas, particularly in Al Hudaydah.
Crop season: As the growing season advances, slight agricultural water stress (ASI: 25–40%) was observed in parts of Dhamar, Al Bayda, and Sana'a governorates, indicating localized soil moisture deficits.
Livelihoods and food security: Below-average rainfall combined with above-average temperatures reduced soil moisture levels, disrupting crop establishment, increasing water demand, intensifying livestock stress, and further undermining agricultural livelihoods across Yemen.
categories: Food and Agriculture Organization of the United Nations

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: Afghanistan: WFP Aghanistan Country Brief, July 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Afghanistan
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

KEY HIGHLIGHTS

• After a prolonged pipeline break, more than 1,600 mt of the approximately 6,000 mt of nutrition commodities expected in the country have arrived, enabling WFP to reach 80,000 children and 25,000 mothers.

• Since early July, funding shortfalls have forced WFP to suspend famine-prevention programmes in hotspot areas, putting 1.5 million people at risk of life-threatening hunger.

• Funding constraints have also forced WFP to suspend cash assistance at all border crossings, leaving vulnerable returning families without critical support.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

• Regional conflicts are exacerbating the humanitarian crisis in Afghanistan, with staple food prices at elevated levels and transport costs tripling due to prolonged border closure with Pakistan, re-routed supply chains due to the conflict in the Middle East, and increased fuel prices. These supply chain pressures are particularly acute for imported specialized nutritious foods (SNFs), with most WFP-supported health clinics currently facing a stock out of these commodities.

• At the same time, Afghanistan is experiencing record levels of acute malnutrition, with 3.7 million children under five and 1.2 million pregnant and breastfeeding women affected in 2026. The shortage of nutrition supplies, combined with funding constraints, means that one million malnourished children will be unable to receive life-saving assistance this year.

• Since early July, WFP's famine-prevention programmes in hotspot areas have been suspended, putting 1.5 million people at heightened risk of life-threatening hunger. Overall, WFP is now forced to turn away 13 million acutely hungry Afghans.

• In addition, funding constraints have compelled WFP to suspend cash assistance for returnees from Iran and Pakistan at all border crossings, leaving vulnerable families without critical support upon arrival.

These supply chain pressures are particularly acute for imported specialized nutritious foods (SNFs), with most WFP-supported health clinics currently facing a stock out of these commodities.
• At the same time, Afghanistan is experiencing record levels of acute malnutrition, with 3.7 million children under five and 1.2 million pregnant and breastfeeding women affected in 2026. The shortage of nutrition supplies, combined with funding constraints, means that one million malnourished children will be unable to receive life-saving assistance this year.

items.creator: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.title: Burkina Faso : HRP 2026 - Suivi périodique de la réponse humanitaire, mai 2026
items.author: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs
items.content:
Country: Burkina Faso
Source: UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Au mois de mai 2026, les partenaires humanitaires ont atteint cumulativement plus de 926 908 personnes, soit 35 % de la cible intersectorielle, dont près de 362 000 personnes vivant dans des zones de besoins de sévérité la plus élevée (sévérité 4), représentant 39 % de l’ensemble des bénéficiaires. Des progrès notables ont été enregistrés dans certaines zones de sévérité 4, notamment dans la région du Liptako avec plus de 87 000 personnes atteintes, dont près de 50 000 dans la province de Seno, à Oudalan (Liptako) avec plus de 17 000 personnes atteintes, et à Karo-Peli (Soum) avec près de 24 000 personnes atteintes. À l’inverse, Gobnangou est resté sous le seuil de 5 % de sa cible au cours des deux premiers mois de l’année, avec respectivement 8 380 personnes atteintes. La majorité des personnes atteintes se trouvent dans des zones de sévérité 3, qui représentent 50 % du total, suivies des zones de sévérité 4 avec 39 %, tandis que près de 102 000 personnes ont également bénéficié d’interventions dans des zones de sévérité 2, non prioritaires dans le cadre du Plan de réponse humanitaire 2026.

items.creator: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.title: UNHCR Ukraine Operational Update - 10 July 2026
items.author: UN High Commissioner for Refugees
items.content:
Country: Ukraine
Source: UN High Commissioner for Refugees

Please refer to the attached file.

OPERATIONAL CONTEXT & HIGHLIGHTS:

• The Russian Federation has significantly intensified aerial attacks against Ukraine, with Kyiv and other cities subjected to multiple large-scale strikes. The UN HRMMU reported that civilian casualties in Ukraine were higher in June 2026 than in any other month since April 2022, with at least 293 civilians killed and 1,990 injured. This concerning trend appears to be continuing into July. A massive attack on Kyiv overnight on 1-2 July killed 31 people. Only a few days later, another attack on 5-6 July killed 19 people in Kyiv city and ten people in the Kyiv region. So far this year, Russian Armed Forces launched over 520 ballistic missiles at Ukraine, more than twice as many as in the same period in 2025, according to Ukraine air force data.

• The Ukraine Recovery Conference (URC) took place in Gdańsk, Poland, on 25 and 26 June. UNHCR’s Representative Bernadette Castel-Hollingsworth attended, joining key discussions with partners, UN agencies and Government counterparts. The Representative also spoke at a side event on recovery and future returns of displaced Ukrainians – highlighting the need for people-centered investments in housing, jobs and services in Ukraine.

• Marking the 75th anniversary of the 1951 Refugee Convention and the 65th anniversary of the 1961 Convention on the Reduction of Statelessness – and ahead of World Refugee Day in June – UNHCR together with the Ministry of Foreign Affairs launched a photo exhibition in Kyiv highlighting the stories of refugees and stateless people in Ukraine.

• In the fifth year since the start of the Russian full-scale invasion of Ukraine, and more than 12 years since the war began, UNHCR’s programme in Ukraine increasingly aims to help war-affected people and communities to recover, and displaced people to find durable solutions, particularly through housing and strategic protection advocacy.

items.creator: Pan American Health Organization
items.title: World: WHO and UNICEF estimates show routine immunization has rebounded in the Americas, but declining measles coverage leaves communities vulnerable to outbreaks
items.author: Pan American Health Organization
items.content:
Country: World
Source: Pan American Health Organization

Washington, D.C., 15 July 2026 (PAHO) – Childhood vaccination in the Americas continued to recover in 2025, with the number of unvaccinated children under the age of one falling from 1.3 million to 1.1 million. However, declining measles vaccination coverage is leaving communities increasingly vulnerable to outbreaks, according to the latest WHO and UNICEF Estimates of National Immunization Coverage (WUENIC), released today.

Progress towards strengthening routine immunization programmes in the region is moving forward: Coverage with the first dose of diphtheria, tetanus and pertussis vaccine (DTP1) reached 92%, while coverage with the third dose (DTP3) held steady at 86%, confirming that the Americas have not only recovered from pandemic-related setbacks but also surpassed pre-pandemic routine immunization levels.

“The Americas have demonstrated that progress is possible. Countries have strengthened routine immunization programmes, reduced the number of children missing lifesaving vaccines, and continue to lead globally in important areas such as HPV vaccination. But the resurgence of measles is a warning that we cannot take these achievements for granted,” said Dr. Jarbas Barbosa, Director of PAHO.

Persistent challenges in measles coverage

Measles vaccination coverage, which increased in 2024, experienced a decline in 2025. Since 2024, coverage with the first dose of measles-containing vaccine (MCV1) has fallen from 89% to 88%, while second-dose coverage (MCV2) has dropped from 79% to 78% - both well below the 95% required to prevent outbreaks of this highly contagious disease.

The estimates also reveal marked differences in coverage both between and within countries. While some have consistently achieved measles vaccination coverage above 90%, others remain well below this threshold, reflecting persistent geographic and socio-economic inequalities in access to immunization services.

Several countries in the region continue to respond to measles outbreaks, most of which have occurred among people who were not vaccinated or who had not received all recommended doses. These outbreaks highlight how quickly measles can spread when immunity gaps persist, underscoring the importance of continued investment in maintaining high vaccination coverage, strong surveillance systems, and rapid outbreak response capacities.

“As long as measles continues to circulate anywhere in the world, no country or community can afford to lower its guard. Vaccination is our strongest protection, and we must continue working to reach every child, close immunity gaps, and maintain the high levels of coverage needed to prevent outbreaks,” added Dr. Jarbas Barbosa.

Measles is one of the most contagious diseases in the world. Even small pockets of under-immunized populations can allow transmission to continue and fuel outbreaks. Achieving and sustaining at least 95% coverage with two doses of a measles-containing vaccine in every community remains essential to interrupt transmission and protect vulnerable populations.

A global leader in HPV vaccination

Alongside continued progress in routine childhood immunization, the Americas remain the global leader in human papillomavirus (HPV) vaccination. Coverage reached 71% in 2025, reflecting sustained political commitment and investment in protecting adolescents against cervical cancer, the second leading cause of cancer death among women in many countries of the Region.

This achievement demonstrates what is possible when countries prioritize immunization, expand equitable access to vaccines, and build programmes that protect people throughout the life course.

Maintaining progress requires sustained investment

The recovery of routine immunization in the Americas marks an important public health milestone, but the latest estimates make clear that the work is far from over. Persistent immunity gaps continue to leave communities vulnerable to vaccine-preventable diseases, particularly measles, and threaten decades of hard-won progress.

PAHO is working closely with countries to strengthen national immunization programs, reach children who have missed routine vaccination, increase confidence in vaccines through clear, evidence-based communication, and reinforce surveillance and outbreak response systems. Through the PAHO Revolving Fund, the Organization also supports countries in securing timely, affordable access to quality-assured vaccines, helping them strengthen immunization programmes, increase coverage, and close immunity gaps.

Maintaining high vaccination coverage in every community will be essential not only to prevent future outbreaks, but also to protect the health gains achieved across the Americas and ensure that every child benefits from the lifesaving protection vaccines provide.

Notes to editors:

Download multimedia content here.

Access the WHO dataset: Global dashboard, country profiles, and additional resources

Access the UNICEF dataset: Overview page, Full datasets, Data visualisation, Regional data visualisation, Country profiles

WUENIC estimates, including historical data, are revised annually as new country data become available. Figures in this release should not be compared against previous years' published reports.

Based on country-reported data, the WHO and UNICEF estimates of national immunization coverage (WUENIC) provide the world’s largest and most comprehensive dataset on immunization trends for vaccinations against 13 diseases given through regular health systems - normally at clinics, community centres, outreach services, or health worker visits. For 2025 data were provided from 185 countries.

WHO and UNICEF are working with Gavi, the Vaccine Alliance and other partners to deliver the global Immunization Agenda 2030 (IA2030), a strategy for all countries and relevant global partners to achieve set goals on preventing diseases through immunization and delivering vaccines to everyone, everywhere, at every age.

News Releases

Immunization

Comprehensive Immunization (CIM)

items.creator: Action contre la Faim France
items.title: Niger : Mécanisme de Réponse Rapide (RRM) - Aperçu juin 2026
items.author: Action contre la Faim France
items.content:
Country: Niger
Sources: Action contre la Faim France, Agency for Technical Cooperation and Development, Danish Refugee Council, Government of Niger, International Rescue Committee, UN Children's Fund, UN Office for the Coordination of Humanitarian Affairs, World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached Infographic.

Le Mécanisme de Réponse Rapide (RRM) est constitué d’un réseau d’acteurs capables de réagir rapidement aux urgences humanitaires grâce au pré-positionnement des intrants de secours, à des processus et procédures convenus et aux capacités dédiées. Le RRM fonctionne en complémentarité avec la réponse humanitaire élargie et les structures étatiques, étant intégré dans l’architecture de coordination établie et faisant parti du Plan de Réponse Humanitaire. L’objectif du RRM est de rendre une assistance immédiate de base aux populations affectées par les conflits armés, les catastrophes naturelles ou les épidémies. Le présent produit d’information est réalisé à partir des données secondaires collectées par les partenaires du consortium sur les activités qu’ils mènent en faveur des populations affectées. Ces derniers renseignent les données de leurs interventions sur la matrice RRM en ligne. Ensuite, elles sont extraites, traitées et comparées mensuellement par l’équipe de coordination du RRM Multisectoriel du Niger afin de réaliser la présente communication.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: Pakistan Inter-Agency Humanitarian Cash Working Group: Minimum Expenditure Basked and Cash Transfer Values analysis report, July 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Pakistan
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

In 2026, the Inter-Agency Humanitarian Cash Working Group (CWG) in Pakistan established a taskforce to develop a report providing members with guidelines for calculating the Minimum Expenditure Basket (MEB) as well as multipurpose and sectoral cash transfer values. The report is intended to guide humanitarian and development partners in the design of cash-based transfer interventions in Pakistan.

The drafting process drew on diverse technical expertise across sectors and was informed by broad stakeholder engagement through the CWG. The process was led by the World Food Programme (WFP), with contributions from taskforce members.

The results of the analysis showcase that the minimum expenditure basket amount is PKR 66,000 for urban households and PKR 65,000 for rural households. As for multipurpose cash transfer values, these are PKR 24,000 for urban households and PKR 23,000 for rural households.

This analysis, finalized in June 2026, was endorsed by the Inter-Sectorial Coordination Group on June 24 and by the Humanitarian Country Team on June 29. It covers the period from June 2026 to May 2027, with provisions for earlier revision should the triggers outlined in the report be met. The CWG will continue to monitor price trends on a monthly basis to assess the need for any revision, ensuring that MEB and cash transfer values remain responsive to changing market conditions and household needs.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: WFP Guatemala Country Brief, July 2026
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Guatemala
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

KEY HIGHLIGHTS

• In June, WFP provided assistance to 2,411 people (93 percent women) through a combination of emergency assistance, capacity strengthening, and malnutrition prevention activities.

• In parallel, WFP prepared and prepositioned over 322,300 food rations (January-June) to enable a rapid, Government-led emergency response.

• Guatemala faces a convergence of acute food insecurity and malnutrition, seasonal stress and heightened climate risk, making early action critical to protect livelihoods, safeguard food consumption and prevent further deterioration in nutrition outcomes.

SITUATION OVERVIEW

• In June 2026, The National Oceanic and Atmospheric Administration (NOAA) announced the onset of El Niño, with forecasts pointing to a 63 percent likelihood of a very strong event by the end of 2026, heightening the risk of climaterelated shocks.

• In Guatemala, the onset of El Niño is typically associated with irregular rainfall, prolonged dry spells, higher temperatures, soil moisture deficits and agricultural drought, particularly in rain-fed areas. These conditions could place additional pressure on staple crops such as maize and beans, particularly among rural households that rely on subsistence agriculture.

• These climate-related risks are unfolding against an already severe food security situation, with more than 3 million people projected to face acute food insecurity through to April 2026 (while the 2026–2027 IPC results are expected to be published in July 2026).

• Nutrition vulnerabilities remain equally persistent and structural. Chronic malnutrition affects around 42 percent of children under five nationwide, while more than 11,400 cases of acute malnutrition and 16 associated deaths among children under five had been reported from January to June 2026. This convergence of seasonal climate stress, food insecurity and persistent nutrition vulnerabilities heightens the risk of longer-term human capital losses.

items.creator: UN Children's Fund
items.title: Humanitarian Action for Children - Ukraine and Refugee Response - 2026 Revision 1 (July 2026)
items.author: UN Children's Fund
items.content:
Countries: Ukraine, Belarus, Bulgaria, Moldova, Poland, Romania
Source: UN Children's Fund

Please refer to the attached file.

Appeal highlights

  • The war in Ukraine has entered in its fifth devastating year, marked by an escalation of unrelenting attacks since April 2025 and the systematic destruction of critical infrastructure. The war continues to endanger children’s lives, disrupt essential services and take a severe toll on mental health. In 2026, an estimated 10.8 million people inside Ukraine, including 2.2 million children and 3.8 million internally displaced people, as well as nearly 546,000 Ukrainian refugees in Belarus, Bulgaria, the Republic of Moldova, Poland and Romania require humanitarian assistance.
  • Children and families in Ukraine and in neighbouring refugee-hosting countries face interconnected crises. Conflict-related destruction and displacement have eroded access to basic services and heightened protection and gender-based violence risks inside Ukraine. Beyond its borders, protracted displacement, social exclusion, psychosocial distress and economic insecurity continue to affect refugee children and their families and strain national systems.
  • UNICEF will pursue life-saving, multisectoral assistance to ensure the protection of children and the rehabilitation of essential services in Ukraine’s most affected regions in the north, east and south, while strengthening national systems in host countries to maintain refugee inclusion in this protracted conflict and expand access to education, protection and mental health support.
items.contentSnippet:
Countries: Ukraine, Belarus, Bulgaria, Moldova, Poland, Romania
Source: UN Children's Fund

Please refer to the attached file.
Appeal highlights
The war in Ukraine has entered in its fifth devastating year, marked by an escalation of unrelenting attacks since April 2025 and the systematic destruction of critical infrastructure. The war continues to endanger children’s lives, disrupt essential services and take a severe toll on mental health. In 2026, an estimated 10.8 million people inside Ukraine, including 2.2 million children and 3.8 million internally displaced people, as well as nearly 546,000 Ukrainian refugees in Belarus, Bulgaria, the Republic of Moldova, Poland and Romania require humanitarian assistance.
Children and families in Ukraine and in neighbouring refugee-hosting countries face interconnected crises. Conflict-related destruction and displacement have eroded access to basic services and heightened protection and gender-based violence risks inside Ukraine. Beyond its borders, protracted displacement, social exclusion, psychosocial distress and economic insecurity continue to affect refugee children and their families and strain national systems.
UNICEF will pursue life-saving, multisectoral assistance to ensure the protection of children and the rehabilitation of essential services in Ukraine’s most affected regions in the north, east and south, while strengthening national systems in host countries to maintain refugee inclusion in this protracted conflict and expand access to education, protection and mental health support.

items.creator: World Food Programme
items.title: Spillover Effects: How the Middle East crisis threatens food security - Focus on Myanmar (Date Released: 9 July 2026 / Reporting period: Week of 29 June)
items.author: World Food Programme
items.content:
Country: Myanmar
Source: World Food Programme

Please refer to the attached file.

Overview

The Iran–United States crisis in 2026 has evolved from a high-intensity conflict into a fragile and uncertain de-escalation phase, but its global repercussions remain significant and ongoing. While recent pauses in military exchanges and indirect negotiations have reduced immediate violence, disruptions to the Strait of Hormuz—through which around one-fifth of global oil supply flows—have already triggered sharp increases in energy prices, supply chain instability, and rising inflation worldwide. These shocks are particularly severe for energy-importing and developing economies, contributing to slower global growth, higher food and commodity prices, and increased economic vulnerability across Asia. Myanmar faces especially deep impacts due to its heavy dependence on imported fuel (around 9095% of supply), limited reserves, and fragile economic conditions; the crisis has led to rising fuel prices, shortages, currency depreciation, and increased costs of transportation, food, and agricultural production. As a result, the conflict is not only a geopolitical issue but also a major economic shock, disproportionately affecting vulnerable countries like Myanmar, where it intensifies inflation, threatens livelihoods, and exacerbates existing economic instability.
